---
title: "Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # generic vectors / input directions
HILITE = "#c1121f"   # eigenvectors / invariant directions
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # second eigenvector / highlights
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"


def draw_vec(ax, tail, head, color=VEC, lw=2.4, label=None, z=5,
             label_offset=(0.0, 0.0), ls="-"):
    """Draw a 2-D arrow from tail to head with a clean head."""
    ax.annotate("", xy=head, xytext=tail,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0, linestyle=ls), zorder=z)
    if label is not None:
        ax.text(head[0] + label_offset[0], head[1] + label_offset[1], label,
                color=color, fontsize=13, ha="center", va="center", zorder=z + 1)
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This opens **Movement IV — Eigenstructure and the
> Spectral Picture**. It assumes you can read a matrix as a map that sends the basis
> vectors to its columns ([GEOM-08]), that you know when $A-\lambda I$ fails to be
> invertible and what a nontrivial null space means ([GEOM-11], [GEOM-12]), and that you
> have met the determinant as the signed-volume factor that vanishes exactly when a map
> collapses space ([GEOM-13]). Here we ask the one question that organizes everything that
> follows: *does a matrix leave any direction pointing the same way, only stretched?* It
> sets up [GEOM-15] (diagonalization — writing $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$ and making powers
> trivial), [GEOM-16] (the spectral theorem, where symmetry makes the picture perfect),
> [GEOM-18] (positive-definite $\Leftrightarrow$ positive eigenvalues), and [GEOM-26] (the
> condition number as a ratio of eigenvalues). It also **discharges Regression's
> eigenvalue/condition-number prerequisite ([REGR-07])**.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A matrix is a map: feed it a vector, it hands back another, usually pointing somewhere
new. This article asks whether any input directions survive the map *un-rotated* — sent
to a scalar multiple of themselves, $A\mathbf v = \lambda\mathbf v$. Such a $\mathbf v$ is
an **eigenvector** and the stretch factor $\lambda$ its **eigenvalue**. We build the
definition from the geometry of [GEOM-08]'s transformation gallery, then turn the
search into algebra: $A\mathbf v = \lambda\mathbf v$ holds for some $\mathbf v\neq 0$
exactly when $A-\lambda I$ is singular, i.e. when $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$ — the
**characteristic polynomial**. We find every root, then read off the eigenvectors as the
null space of $A-\lambda I$, working a $2\times2$ and a $3\times3$ entirely by hand. The
single claim the article defends is that **eigenvectors are the natural coordinate axes of
a linear map** — in those directions the map stops being a tangle of rotation-plus-shear
and becomes pure scaling. We are equally honest about where this fails: rotations have *no*
real eigenvectors, repeated roots can starve a matrix of enough eigenvectors to span the
space, and such **defective** matrices cannot be diagonalized at all. Those limits are the
exact reason the next articles exist.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Most directions, when you hit them with a matrix $A$, come out rotated *and* stretched.
An **eigenvector** is a rare direction that comes out only **stretched** — same line,
possibly flipped, scaled by a number $\lambda$ called its **eigenvalue**:
$$
A\mathbf v = \lambda\mathbf v, \qquad \mathbf v \neq \mathbf 0 .
$$
These special directions are the map's *own* axes. Along them the matrix does nothing but
multiply by a number, so they are where the matrix's behavior is simplest to see, easiest
to compute with, and most revealing of what the map "really does."
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The equation $A\mathbf v = \lambda\mathbf v$ looks like
a definition to memorize, but by the end you should feel *why* it matters: a generic
matrix mixes its coordinates together in a confusing way, and the eigenvectors are exactly
the change of viewpoint that un-mixes them. Find the eigenvectors and you have found the
coordinate system in which $A$ is diagonal — the cleanest description a linear map can
have. That promise is cashed in [GEOM-15]; here we earn the directions themselves.

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forces the question {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **which directions does a matrix leave
alone, and why would we want them?** Every algebraic move below is an answer to some piece
of that.

## What came before, and the gap it leaves

By [GEOM-08] we can read any matrix as a map $\mathbf x \mapsto A\mathbf x$, with the
columns of $A$ telling us where the standard axes land. That view is complete but
*opaque*: knowing where $\mathbf e_1$ and $\mathbf e_2$ go tells you, in principle,
where every vector goes (by linearity), yet it gives almost no feel for the *character* of
the map. Is it a rotation? A stretch? A shear? A collapse? Two matrices that look totally
different in their columns can do nearly the same geometric thing, and two that look
similar can behave wildly differently. The column picture answers "where does each axis
go" but not "what is this map *like*."

There is also a concrete computational pain. Suppose a process repeats a linear step over
and over — a population redistributing each year, a Markov chain taking another step, an
iterative numerical method grinding forward, the repeated application that the web's
original PageRank reduced to. The state after $k$ steps is $A^k\mathbf x_0$. Computing
$A^{100}$ by multiplying $A$ a hundred times is both expensive and uninformative: the
entries churn into a mess that hides where the process is heading. We want a way to *see
through* the repeated map to its long-run behavior, and to compute $A^k$ cheaply.

## The idea that cracks both problems open

Both pains have the same cure. Suppose — just suppose — there were directions $\mathbf v$
in which $A$ acted as plain multiplication by a number:
$$
A\mathbf v = \lambda\mathbf v .
$$
Then applying $A$ again is trivial: $A^2\mathbf v = A(\lambda\mathbf v) = \lambda
A\mathbf v = \lambda^2\mathbf v$, and in general $A^k\mathbf v = \lambda^k\mathbf v$. The
hundredfold map, restricted to such a direction, is just multiplication by
$\lambda^{100}$ — no matrix products at all. And if enough such directions exist to build
*any* starting vector out of them, then the behavior of $A$ (and of $A^k$) on *every*
vector reduces to scaling along each special direction separately. The confusing,
coordinate-mixing map dissolves into a handful of independent one-dimensional stretches.

That "if enough such directions exist" is the whole drama of Movement IV, and we will be
honest about when it does and does not hold. But the prize is clear enough to justify the
hunt: **find the directions a matrix only stretches, and you have found the coordinate
system in which the matrix is simplest.** This article finds them. [GEOM-15] assembles
them into the decomposition $A = V\Lambda V^{-1}$ that makes $A^k = V\Lambda^k V^{-1}$.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In one sentence, why do we care about eigenvectors?"* Because they are the directions in
which a linear map is just multiplication by a number, so in the coordinate system built
from them the matrix becomes diagonal — and a diagonal matrix is one you can read, raise
to powers, invert, and reason about at a glance. Every downstream use (PCA's principal
axes, a covariance matrix's directions of variance, the convergence rate of an optimizer,
the stability of a dynamical system) is some specialization of that one sentence.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Take the cleanest possible non-trivial map and *watch* it act on a ring of input
directions. Consider
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix},
$$
a shear-and-stretch. Send a whole circle of unit vectors through it. Most arrows come out
both rotated and lengthened — the output arrow points somewhere different from the input.
But two input directions are special: they come out pointing along the *same line* they
went in on, merely longer. @fig-eigen-action shows this directly. The input directions are
drawn faint; the same directions after the map are drawn bold; an arrow is colored red
exactly when the output lies on the input's own line.

```{python}
#| label: fig-eigen-action
#| fig-cap: 'A matrix acting on a ring of input directions. Faint grey arrows are unit inputs; bold arrows are their images under $A=\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}2&1\\0&3\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$. Almost every direction is *rotated* by the map (its image points off the input''s line). Two directions are not: the red horizontal eigenvector (eigenvalue $2$) and the green diagonal eigenvector (eigenvalue $3$) are sent straight back out along their own lines, only stretched. Those are the eigenvectors.'
A = np.array([[2.0, 1.0], [0.0, 3.0]])

thetas = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 24, endpoint=False)
units = np.stack([np.cos(thetas), np.sin(thetas)])  # 2 x 24
imgs = A @ units

# Eigenvectors of this A: lambda=2 -> [1,0]; lambda=3 -> [1,1]/sqrt(2)
ev1 = np.array([1.0, 0.0])
ev2 = np.array([1.0, 1.0]) / np.sqrt(2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6, 5.6))
for k in range(units.shape[1]):
    u = units[:, k]
    img = imgs[:, k]
    # is the image collinear with the input? cross product near zero
    collinear = abs(u[0] * img[1] - u[1] * img[0]) < 1e-9
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), u, color=PALE, lw=1.4, z=2)
    col = HILITE if collinear and u[0] >= 0 and abs(u[1]) < 1e-9 else (
        ACCENT if collinear and u[0] > 0 and u[1] > 0 else GREY)
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), img, color=col, lw=2.2, z=4)

draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), 3.2 * ev1, color=HILITE, lw=1.0, ls=":", z=1)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), 3.2 * ev2, color=ACCENT, lw=1.0, ls=":", z=1)
ax.text(2.9, -0.35, r"$\lambda=2$", color=HILITE, fontsize=12)
ax.text(2.1, 2.5, r"$\lambda=3$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=12)
ax.set_xlim(-3.6, 3.6); ax.set_ylim(-3.6, 3.6)
ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.axhline(0, color="k", lw=0.6); ax.axvline(0, color="k", lw=0.6)
ax.set_title("Most directions rotate; two only stretch")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Stare at the picture and the definition writes itself. An input $\mathbf v$ is special
when its image $A\mathbf v$ lies on the same line through the origin as $\mathbf v$. "On
the same line, through the origin" means "a scalar multiple": $A\mathbf v = \lambda
\mathbf v$ for some number $\lambda$. The horizontal direction comes out twice as long
($\lambda = 2$); the diagonal direction comes out three times as long ($\lambda = 3$).
Every other direction gets knocked off its own line.

Three quick observations we will make rigorous below:

- **It is about a line, not a single arrow.** If $A\mathbf v = \lambda\mathbf v$, then for
  any scalar $c\neq 0$, $A(c\mathbf v) = c\,A\mathbf v = c\lambda\mathbf v =
  \lambda(c\mathbf v)$. So $c\mathbf v$ is an eigenvector too, with the *same* $\lambda$.
  An eigenvector is really an **eigen-direction** (a whole line); the specific length is
  ours to choose, and we usually normalize to length $1$.
- **$\lambda$ can be negative or zero.** Negative $\lambda$ flips the arrow to the
  opposite ray of the same line (still the same line). $\lambda = 0$ means $A\mathbf v =
  \mathbf 0$: the direction is crushed to the origin — those eigenvectors are exactly the
  null space of $A$ ([GEOM-12]).
- **There may be none (real ones).** A pure rotation turns *every* direction off its line,
  so it has no real eigenvectors at all. We will meet this honestly in @sec-rotation.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why must an eigenvector be nonzero, when $A\mathbf 0 = \lambda\mathbf 0$ holds for every
$\lambda$?"* Exactly because it holds for *every* $\lambda$. The zero vector satisfies
$A\mathbf 0 = \lambda\mathbf 0$ trivially for all $\lambda$, so it would make every number
an eigenvalue and carry no information about $A$. We exclude it by fiat in the definition.
Eigen*values*, by contrast, are allowed to be zero — that is a genuine, informative case
($A$ collapses that direction).
:::

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## The definition {#sec-def}

::: {#def-eigen}
**Definition (eigenvalue, eigenvector).** Let $A$ be an $n\times n$ (square) matrix. A
scalar $\lambda$ is an **eigenvalue** of $A$ if there exists a *nonzero* vector
$\mathbf v\in\mathbb R^n$ with
$$
A\mathbf v = \lambda\mathbf v .
$$ {#eq-eigen}
Any such $\mathbf v$ is an **eigenvector** of $A$ associated with $\lambda$. The set of
all eigenvectors for a fixed $\lambda$, together with $\mathbf 0$, is the **eigenspace** of
$\lambda$, written $E_\lambda$.
:::

Every symbol earns its place. $A$ must be **square** ($n\times n$): @eq-eigen demands
$A\mathbf v$ and $\mathbf v$ live in the same space so we can compare them, which forces
the map to send $\mathbb R^n$ to $\mathbb R^n$. (Rectangular matrices have an analogous but
distinct theory — *singular* values — built in [GEOM-20]; do not conflate the two.) The
scalar $\lambda$ is the stretch factor; the nonzero $\mathbf v$ is the surviving direction.

We should immediately confirm that the "eigenspace" really is a space and not just a
loose collection of arrows, because we will reason about it as a subspace throughout.

::: {#lem-eigenspace}
**Lemma (the eigenspace is a subspace).** For a fixed eigenvalue $\lambda$ of $A$, the set
$$
E_\lambda = \{\mathbf v \in \mathbb R^n : A\mathbf v = \lambda\mathbf v\}
$$
is a subspace of $\mathbb R^n$ (closed under addition and scalar multiplication, and
containing $\mathbf 0$).
:::

::: {.proof}
We check the two closure properties; containing $\mathbf 0$ is immediate since $A\mathbf 0
= \mathbf 0 = \lambda\mathbf 0$.

*Closed under scaling.* Take $\mathbf v\in E_\lambda$ and a scalar $c$. Using linearity of
the map (the rule $A(c\mathbf v)=cA\mathbf v$ from [GEOM-08]):
$$
A(c\mathbf v) = c\,A\mathbf v = c\,(\lambda\mathbf v) = \lambda\,(c\mathbf v),
$$
so $c\mathbf v\in E_\lambda$. (This is the "it's a whole line" observation made precise.)

*Closed under addition.* Take $\mathbf v,\mathbf w\in E_\lambda$. Using
$A(\mathbf v+\mathbf w)=A\mathbf v+A\mathbf w$ (also from [GEOM-08]):
$$
A(\mathbf v+\mathbf w) = A\mathbf v + A\mathbf w
= \lambda\mathbf v + \lambda\mathbf w = \lambda(\mathbf v+\mathbf w),
$$
so $\mathbf v+\mathbf w\in E_\lambda$. Both closures hold, so $E_\lambda$ is a subspace.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is more than tidiness. It says that for a *repeated* eigenvalue the eigenvectors can
fill a whole plane or higher-dimensional flat, not just a line — a fact we will need when
we discuss whether there are "enough" eigenvectors (@sec-defective).

## Turning the definition into something we can solve {#sec-rearrange}

@eq-eigen has *two* unknowns tangled together — the scalar $\lambda$ and the vector
$\mathbf v$ — and that is what makes it look hard. The decisive move is to get all the
matrix action onto one side. Subtract $\lambda\mathbf v$ from both sides:
$$
A\mathbf v - \lambda\mathbf v = \mathbf 0 .
$$
We would like to factor out $\mathbf v$, but $A\mathbf v - \lambda\mathbf v$ is "matrix
times vector minus scalar times vector," and a scalar and a matrix cannot be subtracted
directly. The fix is the **identity matrix** $I$ (the $n\times n$ matrix with $1$'s on the
diagonal and $0$'s elsewhere), which by definition satisfies $I\mathbf v = \mathbf v$ for
every $\mathbf v$. Rewrite $\lambda\mathbf v = \lambda I\mathbf v = (\lambda I)\mathbf v$:
$$
A\mathbf v - (\lambda I)\mathbf v = \mathbf 0 .
$$
Now both terms are "(a matrix) times $\mathbf v$," so we factor $\mathbf v$ out on the
right (the distributive law for matrix-vector products, $B\mathbf v - C\mathbf v = (B-C)
\mathbf v$, which follows entrywise):
$$
(A - \lambda I)\,\mathbf v = \mathbf 0 .
$$ {#eq-homog}

This is a major simplification. @eq-homog is a **homogeneous linear system**: the matrix
$A-\lambda I$ times the unknown vector $\mathbf v$ gives the zero vector. We met exactly
this kind of equation in [GEOM-11] and [GEOM-12]. And now the question "is $\lambda$ an
eigenvalue?" becomes a question about that system.

## The condition on $\lambda$: a singular matrix {#sec-singular}

@eq-homog *always* has the solution $\mathbf v = \mathbf 0$. But the definition demands a
**nonzero** $\mathbf v$. So $\lambda$ is an eigenvalue precisely when the system
$(A-\lambda I)\mathbf v = \mathbf 0$ has a solution *other than* the zero vector — i.e.
when it has a **nontrivial null space**.

When does a square system $M\mathbf v = \mathbf 0$ have a nonzero solution? From
[GEOM-11]–[GEOM-12], the following are equivalent for a square matrix $M$: it has a
nontrivial null space $\Leftrightarrow$ its columns are linearly dependent
$\Leftrightarrow$ it is *not* invertible (singular) $\Leftrightarrow$ it does not have full
rank. Apply this with $M = A-\lambda I$:
$$
\lambda \text{ is an eigenvalue of } A
\iff
A - \lambda I \text{ is singular (not invertible).}
$$ {#eq-singular-cond}

We have traded one hard question (find $\lambda$ *and* $\mathbf v$ together) for two
easier ones in sequence: **first** find the $\lambda$'s that make $A-\lambda I$ singular,
**then**, for each such $\lambda$, find the null space to get the eigenvectors. The first
step needs a test for singularity that we can actually compute as a function of $\lambda$.
That test is the determinant.

## The characteristic polynomial {#sec-charpoly}

In [GEOM-13] the determinant earned its meaning as the signed volume scaling of a map, and
its headline property is that it vanishes exactly when the map collapses space — that is,
**$\det M = 0 \iff M$ is singular.** Combine that with @eq-singular-cond:
$$
\boxed{\;\lambda \text{ is an eigenvalue of } A \iff \det(A-\lambda I) = 0.\;}
$$ {#eq-char-eq}

The left side is geometry (a surviving direction); the right side is a single scalar
equation we can solve. Give the function a name.

::: {#def-charpoly}
**Definition (characteristic polynomial).** For an $n\times n$ matrix $A$, the
**characteristic polynomial** is
$$
p_A(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I) .
$$
The equation $p_A(\lambda)=0$ is the **characteristic equation**; its roots are exactly the
eigenvalues of $A$.
:::

Why call it a *polynomial*? Because the determinant of an $n\times n$ matrix is a sum of
products, each product taking exactly one entry from each row and column ([GEOM-13]). In
$A-\lambda I$, the variable $\lambda$ appears only on the diagonal, as $a_{ii}-\lambda$. So
$\lambda$ enters each product at most $n$ times (once per diagonal entry it touches), and
the whole determinant is a polynomial in $\lambda$ of degree exactly $n$. We will see the
degree-$n$ claim concretely in the worked $2\times2$ next, and state the general structure
right after.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why does $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$, rather than $\det(\lambda I - A)=0$ — and does the sign
matter?"* They have the *same roots*. Since $\lambda I - A = -(A-\lambda I)$ and scaling an
$n\times n$ matrix by $-1$ multiplies its determinant by $(-1)^n$ ([GEOM-13]), the two
characteristic polynomials differ only by the nonzero factor $(-1)^n$, which never changes
where the polynomial equals zero. Texts pick whichever makes the leading coefficient
$+1$: using $\det(\lambda I - A)$ gives a *monic* polynomial $\lambda^n - \cdots$, which is
why it is common. We will use $\det(A-\lambda I)$ for derivations and not worry about the
overall sign, since only the roots matter.
:::

## A worked $2\times2$, fully symbolic {#sec-2x2-symbolic}

Let $A=\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d\end{bmatrix}$. Then
$$
A - \lambda I
= \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d\end{bmatrix}
- \begin{bmatrix} \lambda & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} a-\lambda & b \\ c & d-\lambda\end{bmatrix}.
$$
The $2\times2$ determinant is (top-left times bottom-right, minus the other diagonal — the
formula from [GEOM-13]):
$$
p_A(\lambda) = \det(A-\lambda I) = (a-\lambda)(d-\lambda) - bc .
$$
Expand the product, being careful with every sign:
$$
(a-\lambda)(d-\lambda) = ad - a\lambda - d\lambda + \lambda^2
= \lambda^2 - (a+d)\lambda + ad .
$$
Subtract $bc$:
$$
p_A(\lambda) = \lambda^2 - (a+d)\,\lambda + (ad - bc) .
$$ {#eq-2x2-charpoly}

Read the coefficients — they are not random, and recognizing them is worth genuine points
on an exam:

- The constant term $ad-bc$ is exactly $\det A$ (set $\lambda=0$: $p_A(0)=\det(A-0)=\det
  A$). So **the product of the roots equals $\det A$** (for a monic quadratic
  $\lambda^2 - s\lambda + p$, the product of roots is $p$).
- The coefficient $a+d$ is the **trace** of $A$ — the sum of its diagonal entries, written
  $\operatorname{tr}A$. So **the sum of the roots equals $\operatorname{tr}A$** (sum of
  roots of a monic quadratic is $s$).

These two facts — $\sum\lambda_i = \operatorname{tr}A$ and $\prod\lambda_i = \det A$ — hold
for every $n$, not just $2\times2$, and they are the fastest sanity check in all of
eigenvalue computation. We state the general version once we have the general structure.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Without computing eigenvectors, how do you check your eigenvalues are right?"* Add them
and compare to the trace; multiply them and compare to the determinant. If $A=
\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}2&1\\0&3\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$ and you found $\lambda=2,3$:
their sum is $5=\operatorname{tr}A=2+3$ ✓, and their product is $6=\det A = 2\cdot3-1\cdot0$
✓. This two-second check catches most arithmetic slips and is the kind of fluency
interviewers probe for.
:::

## The general structure: $n$ roots, counted with multiplicity {#sec-fundamental}

For general $n$, expanding $\det(A-\lambda I)$ gives a degree-$n$ polynomial
$$
p_A(\lambda) = (-1)^n\bigl(\lambda^n - (\operatorname{tr}A)\,\lambda^{n-1}
+ \cdots + (-1)^n\det A\bigr),
$$
and the eigenvalues are its roots. How many roots does a degree-$n$ polynomial have? Here
we must import one named result, because it sits above our Calc-1 floor and is genuinely a
theorem of algebra, not of geometry.

::: {.callout-note title="What we borrow: the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra"}
**Fundamental Theorem of Algebra.** Every polynomial of degree $n\ge 1$ with real (or
complex) coefficients has exactly $n$ roots in the **complex numbers**
$\mathbb C$, counted with multiplicity. Equivalently, it factors completely as
$p(\lambda) = c\,(\lambda-\lambda_1)(\lambda-\lambda_2)\cdots(\lambda-\lambda_n)$ over
$\mathbb C$.

This is a foundational result of algebra/analysis; we use only its *statement*. The key
consequence for us: **an $n\times n$ matrix has exactly $n$ eigenvalues over $\mathbb C$,
counted with multiplicity** — but some may be complex, and some may repeat. The two
"defects" this allows (complex roots, repeated roots) are precisely the honest
complications of @sec-complications.
:::

Two pieces of vocabulary we need for the rest of the article:

- The **algebraic multiplicity** of an eigenvalue $\lambda_0$ is the number of times the
  factor $(\lambda-\lambda_0)$ appears in $p_A$ — how many times it is repeated as a root.
- The **geometric multiplicity** of $\lambda_0$ is $\dim E_{\lambda_0}$, the dimension of
  its eigenspace — how many *independent* eigenvectors it actually supplies.

The gap between these two numbers is the entire subject of @sec-defective and the reason
[GEOM-15] is a separate article. For now, the general trace/determinant identities:

::: {#thm-trace-det}
**Theorem (sum and product of eigenvalues).** If $A$ is $n\times n$ with eigenvalues
$\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n$ (over $\mathbb C$, counted with multiplicity), then
$$
\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i = \operatorname{tr}A, \qquad \prod_{i=1}^n \lambda_i = \det A .
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
We first pin down the factored form *with its exact sign*, since the whole proof rides on
it. The Fundamental Theorem (in the callout above) factors any degree-$n$ polynomial as
$p_A(\lambda) = c\,(\lambda-\lambda_1)\cdots(\lambda-\lambda_n)$, where $\lambda_1,\dots,
\lambda_n$ are its roots — the eigenvalues — and $c$ is the leading coefficient (the
coefficient of $\lambda^n$). We need that $c$. Look at where the highest power of $\lambda$
can come from in $\det(A-\lambda I)$: the determinant is a sum of products, each product
picking one entry from every row and column ([GEOM-13]), and $\lambda$ appears only on the
diagonal of $A-\lambda I$, as $a_{ii}-\lambda$. To collect all $n$ powers of $\lambda$ we
must take the diagonal entry from *every* row, i.e. the single product
$\prod_{i=1}^n(a_{ii}-\lambda)$; any product that strays off the diagonal even once picks up
at most $n-1$ factors of $\lambda$. The $\lambda^n$ term of $\prod_i(a_{ii}-\lambda)$ is
$(-\lambda)^n=(-1)^n\lambda^n$, so the leading coefficient is
$$
c = (-1)^n .
$$
Therefore $p_A(\lambda) = (-1)^n\prod_{i=1}^n(\lambda-\lambda_i)$. Pulling one factor of
$-1$ into each of the $n$ binomials — $(-1)(\lambda-\lambda_i) = (\lambda_i-\lambda)$, and
there are exactly $n$ of them, absorbing the whole $(-1)^n$ — gives the sign-clean form we
will use:
$$
\det(A-\lambda I) = (-1)^n\prod_{i=1}^n(\lambda-\lambda_i) = \prod_{i=1}^n (\lambda_i - \lambda).
$$
*Product.* Set $\lambda = 0$: the left side is $\det(A-0\cdot I)=\det A$, the right side is
$\prod_i (\lambda_i - 0)=\prod_i \lambda_i$. Hence $\det A = \prod_i\lambda_i$.

*Sum.* Expand the right side and track the coefficient of $\lambda^{n-1}$. Each factor
contributes either $\lambda_i$ or $-\lambda$; a term of degree $n-1$ in $\lambda$ comes
from choosing $-\lambda$ in $n-1$ factors and the constant $\lambda_j$ in the one remaining
factor, summed over which factor is left out. Writing $[\lambda^{n-1}]P$ for "the
coefficient of $\lambda^{n-1}$ in the polynomial $P$,"
$$
[\lambda^{n-1}]\prod_i(\lambda_i-\lambda)
= \sum_{j=1}^n \lambda_j \cdot (-1)^{n-1}.
$$
On the left side, $\det(A-\lambda I)$: the only way to get a $\lambda^{n-1}$ term in the
determinant expansion is the product of all $n$ diagonal entries $\prod_i(a_{ii}-\lambda)$
(any off-diagonal choice kills at least two of the $-\lambda$'s, dropping the degree below
$n-1$). Expanding $\prod_i(a_{ii}-\lambda)$ the same way, its $\lambda^{n-1}$ coefficient is
$\sum_i a_{ii}\cdot(-1)^{n-1} = (\operatorname{tr}A)(-1)^{n-1}$. Matching the two
coefficients and cancelling the common $(-1)^{n-1}$ gives $\sum_j\lambda_j =
\operatorname{tr}A$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

## Finding the eigenvectors once you have $\lambda$ {#sec-find-vecs}

The eigenvalues are the roots of $p_A$; the eigenvectors come from @eq-homog. For each
eigenvalue $\lambda_k$, the eigenvectors are precisely the **nonzero vectors in the null
space of $A-\lambda_k I$**:
$$
E_{\lambda_k} = \operatorname{null}(A-\lambda_k I) = \{\mathbf v : (A-\lambda_k I)\mathbf v
= \mathbf 0\}.
$$
We compute that null space exactly as in [GEOM-11]: substitute the number $\lambda_k$ into
$A-\lambda I$, row-reduce, and read off the free directions. Because $\lambda_k$ was chosen
to make $A-\lambda_k I$ singular, this null space is *guaranteed* to be nontrivial — there
will always be at least one free variable, hence at least one eigenvector. We do this
concretely in the worked examples next.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Eigenvectors are never unique, and that is not a mistake.** Because $E_\lambda$ is a
subspace (@lem-eigenspace), any nonzero scalar multiple of an eigenvector is also an
eigenvector for the same $\lambda$, and if the eigenspace is multidimensional any
combination of a basis works. When a textbook or a library reports "the eigenvector," it
has silently picked a *normalization* — usually unit length, $\|\mathbf v\|=1$, and even
then the sign $\pm\mathbf v$ is a free choice. If your answer is a scalar multiple of the
book's, you are not wrong. Reporting an eigenvector means reporting a *direction* (a basis
for the eigenspace), not a unique arrow.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now compute eigenvalues and eigenvectors on real matrices, by hand first so the
machinery is fully exposed, then on larger and messier objects where the geometry carries
real meaning. We deliberately include the honest edge cases — a rotation with no real
eigenvectors and a defective matrix that cannot be diagonalized — so the boundary of the
technique is visible, not hidden.

## Example 1 — a $2\times2$, every step by hand {#sec-ex-2x2}

Take
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix},
$$
the symmetric matrix that appears constantly in data work (it is the structure of a
$2\times2$ covariance matrix with equal variances and positive correlation). We find its
eigenstructure with nothing but @eq-2x2-charpoly and a null-space computation.

**Step 1 — characteristic polynomial.** Using @eq-2x2-charpoly with $a=d=2$, $b=c=1$:
$$
p_A(\lambda) = \lambda^2 - (a+d)\lambda + (ad-bc)
= \lambda^2 - 4\lambda + (4-1) = \lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 3 .
$$

**Step 2 — roots (eigenvalues).** Factor the quadratic: $\lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 3 =
(\lambda-1)(\lambda-3)$, so
$$
\lambda_1 = 1, \qquad \lambda_2 = 3 .
$$
Sanity check via @thm-trace-det: sum $1+3=4=\operatorname{tr}A$ ✓; product $1\cdot3=3=\det
A = 4-1$ ✓.

**Step 3 — eigenvector for $\lambda_1=1$.** Form $A-1\cdot I$ and find its null space:
$$
A - I = \begin{bmatrix} 2-1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2-1\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1\end{bmatrix}.
$$
Solve $(A-I)\mathbf v = \mathbf 0$, i.e. $\begin{bmatrix}1&1\\1&1\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}v_1\\v_2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0\\0\end{bmatrix}$. Both rows say the
same thing, $v_1 + v_2 = 0$, so $v_1 = -v_2$. Pick the free variable $v_2 = 1$:
$$
\mathbf v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\quad\text{(or any nonzero multiple).}
$$
Verify: $A\mathbf v_1 = \begin{bmatrix}2&1\\1&2\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}-1\\1\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix}-2+1\\-1+2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}-1\\1\end{bmatrix} = 1\cdot
\mathbf v_1$ ✓.

**Step 4 — eigenvector for $\lambda_2=3$.** Form $A-3I$:
$$
A - 3I = \begin{bmatrix} 2-3 & 1 \\ 1 & 2-3\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1\end{bmatrix}.
$$
The system $(A-3I)\mathbf v = \mathbf 0$ gives $-v_1 + v_2 = 0$, so $v_1 = v_2$. Pick
$v_2 = 1$:
$$
\mathbf v_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Verify: $A\mathbf v_2 = \begin{bmatrix}2+1\\1+2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}3\\3\end{bmatrix}
= 3\,\mathbf v_2$ ✓.

Notice the eigenvectors $\begin{bmatrix}-1\\1\end{bmatrix}$ and
$\begin{bmatrix}1\\1\end{bmatrix}$ are **perpendicular** (their dot product is
$-1\cdot1+1\cdot1=0$). That is not luck: it is the spectral theorem at work — *symmetric*
matrices always have orthogonal eigenvectors — and it is the subject of [GEOM-16].
@fig-ex1 confirms the whole computation visually and numerically.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex1
#| fig-cap: 'Example 1, the symmetric matrix $A=\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}2&1\\1&2\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$. The two eigenvectors (red, eigenvalue $1$; green, eigenvalue $3$) are drawn as faint dotted lines; the bold arrows are their images under $A$. Each image lies exactly on its eigenvector''s own line — the red one unchanged in length (stretch $1$), the green one tripled (stretch $3$). The unit circle (grey) maps to the ellipse (blue), whose axes are precisely the eigenvector directions: symmetric matrices map circles to axis-aligned-in-the-eigenbasis ellipses.'
A = np.array([[2.0, 1.0], [1.0, 2.0]])
w, V = np.linalg.eig(A)            # numerical check of our by-hand answer

th = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 200)
circle = np.stack([np.cos(th), np.sin(th)])
ellipse = A @ circle

v1 = np.array([-1.0, 1.0]) / np.sqrt(2)   # lambda = 1
v2 = np.array([1.0, 1.0]) / np.sqrt(2)    # lambda = 3

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6, 5.6))
ax.plot(circle[0], circle[1], color=GREY, lw=1.2, label="unit circle")
ax.plot(ellipse[0], ellipse[1], color=PALE, lw=2.0, label=r"image $A\cdot$circle")
for v, lam, col in [(v1, 1, HILITE), (v2, 3, ACCENT)]:
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), 3.2 * v, color=col, lw=1.0, ls=":", z=1)
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), -3.2 * v, color=col, lw=1.0, ls=":", z=1)
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), v, color=col, lw=1.6, z=4)        # the eigenvector
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), A @ v, color=col, lw=2.8, z=5)    # its image = lam * v
ax.set_xlim(-3.6, 3.6); ax.set_ylim(-3.6, 3.6); ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.axhline(0, color="k", lw=0.6); ax.axvline(0, color="k", lw=0.6)
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
ax.set_title("Eigenvectors are the axes of the image ellipse")
plt.tight_layout()
print("by hand:  eigenvalues 1, 3")
print(f"numpy:    eigenvalues {np.sort(w).round(4)}")
print(f"numpy eigenvectors (columns):\n{V.round(4)}")
```

The printed `numpy` output matches our hand computation up to the usual sign/normalization
freedom flagged in the Pitfall above: the library returns unit-length eigenvectors, and may
return $-\mathbf v$ where we wrote $\mathbf v$.

## Example 2 — a $3\times3$ with a clean factorization {#sec-ex-3x3}

The mechanics scale to any size; only the polynomial gets longer. Take an upper-triangular
matrix because its eigenvalues can be read off directly — and that fact is itself worth
proving, since triangular systems are everywhere (LU, QR, Cholesky).

::: {#lem-triangular}
**Lemma (eigenvalues of a triangular matrix).** If $A$ is triangular (all entries below —
or all above — the main diagonal are zero), its eigenvalues are exactly its diagonal
entries.
:::

::: {.proof}
If $A$ is triangular then so is $A-\lambda I$ (subtracting $\lambda$ from the diagonal
leaves the zero pattern intact). The determinant of a triangular matrix is the product of
its diagonal entries ([GEOM-13]). Hence
$$
p_A(\lambda) = \det(A-\lambda I) = \prod_{i=1}^n (a_{ii}-\lambda),
$$
whose roots are $\lambda = a_{11}, a_{22},\dots,a_{nn}$ — the diagonal entries.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Consider
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
By @lem-triangular the eigenvalues are the diagonal entries $4, 1, 1$ — so $\lambda=1$ is
repeated (algebraic multiplicity $2$). This will let us *see* the algebraic-vs-geometric
multiplicity question concretely. We find the eigenvectors directly.

**$\lambda = 4$.** Compute $A - 4I$ and solve $(A-4I)\mathbf v=\mathbf 0$:
$$
A - 4I = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & -3 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Row 3 gives $-3v_3 = 0\Rightarrow v_3=0$. Row 1 gives $2v_2=0\Rightarrow v_2=0$. Row 2 then
holds automatically. $v_1$ is free; take $v_1=1$:
$$
\mathbf v_{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

**$\lambda = 1$ (the repeated one).** Compute $A - I$ and solve:
$$
A - I = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Row 2 gives $3v_3=0\Rightarrow v_3=0$. Row 1 gives $3v_1+2v_2=0\Rightarrow
v_1=-\tfrac23 v_2$. So $v_2$ is the single free variable; take $v_2=3$ (to clear the
fraction):
$$
\mathbf v_{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ 3 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Here is the crucial observation. The eigenvalue $\lambda=1$ has algebraic multiplicity
**$2$** (it is a double root) but geometric multiplicity **$1$** (its eigenspace is a
single line — only one free variable). The matrix is "one eigenvector short": with only two
independent eigenvectors total ($\mathbf v_{(4)}$ and $\mathbf v_{(1)}$) it cannot supply a
basis of $\mathbb R^3$. This is a **defective** matrix — the precise obstruction to
diagonalization, which @sec-defective dissects and [GEOM-15] resolves.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex3x3
#| fig-cap: 'Example 2 in 3-D: the matrix $A$ with eigenvalues $4,1,1$. The eigenvector for $\lambda=4$ (red) is sent to four times its length; the single eigenvector for the doubly-repeated $\lambda=1$ (green) is fixed in place. There is no third independent eigenvector — the matrix is *defective*. The dashed grey arrow is a generic vector that is *not* an eigenvector, shown rotating off its line under the map.'
A = np.array([[4.0, 2.0, 0.0],
              [0.0, 1.0, 3.0],
              [0.0, 0.0, 1.0]])
w, V = np.linalg.eig(A)

v4 = np.array([1.0, 0.0, 0.0])
v1 = np.array([-2.0, 3.0, 0.0]); v1 = v1 / np.linalg.norm(v1)
g = np.array([0.4, 0.4, 0.8]); g = g / np.linalg.norm(g)   # generic, not an eigenvector

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(6.2, 5.4))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection="3d")
def arr(a, b, color, lw, ls="-"):
    ax.quiver(0, 0, 0, b[0]-a[0], b[1]-a[1], b[2]-a[2], color=color, lw=lw,
              arrow_length_ratio=0.12, linestyle=ls)
arr((0,0,0), v4, HILITE, 1.4, ":"); arr((0,0,0), A@v4/4, HILITE, 2.6)  # show direction
arr((0,0,0), A@v4, HILITE, 1.0, ":")
arr((0,0,0), v1, ACCENT, 2.6)
arr((0,0,0), A@v1, ACCENT, 1.0, ":")
arr((0,0,0), g, GREY, 1.8, "--"); arr((0,0,0), A@g/np.linalg.norm(A@g)*2, GREY, 1.0, ":")
ax.text(*(1.05*v4), r"$v_{\lambda=4}$", color=HILITE)
ax.text(*(1.1*v1), r"$v_{\lambda=1}$", color=ACCENT)
ax.text(*(1.1*g), "generic", color=GREY)
ax.set_xlim(-1, 4); ax.set_ylim(-1, 4); ax.set_zlim(-1, 3)
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$y$"); ax.set_zlabel("$z$")
ax.set_title("Two eigen-directions in 3-D — one short of a basis")
ax.view_init(elev=22, azim=-60)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"eigenvalues (numpy): {np.sort(w).round(4)}")
print("algebraic mult of lambda=1: 2   |   geometric mult (eigenspace dim): 1  -> defective")
```

## Example 3 — a real data matrix: eigenvectors as directions of variance {#sec-ex-cov}

The most important place eigenvectors appear in data science is the **covariance matrix**.
A covariance matrix $\Sigma$ is symmetric and summarizes how a cloud of data points
spreads. Its eigenvectors are the *principal axes* of the cloud — the directions of
greatest and least spread — and its eigenvalues are the variances along those axes. This is
the linear-algebra heart of PCA (whose *statistical* interpretation belongs to Unsupervised
Learning, which links back here for the machinery). We do not assume the probabilistic
meaning; we treat $\Sigma$ as a concrete symmetric matrix and read its geometry.

We generate a correlated 2-D cloud, form its sample covariance matrix, and compute its
eigenstructure. The eigenvectors should land on the visible long and short axes of the
cloud.

```{python}
#| label: fig-cov-eigen
#| fig-cap: 'Eigenvectors of a covariance matrix are the principal axes of the data cloud. A correlated cloud of $n=400$ points (grey) has a $2\times2$ sample covariance matrix $\Sigma$. Its eigenvectors (red = largest eigenvalue, green = smallest) point along the directions of greatest and least spread; each is drawn scaled by $\sqrt{\lambda}$, the standard deviation along that axis. The dotted ellipse is the one-standard-deviation contour, whose axes *are* the eigenvectors. This is exactly the picture PCA exploits.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
# A genuinely correlated cloud (not rigged): tilt + unequal spread.
base = rng.normal(size=(400, 2)) * np.array([3.0, 1.0])
theta = np.deg2rad(30)
R = np.array([[np.cos(theta), -np.sin(theta)],
              [np.sin(theta),  np.cos(theta)]])
X = base @ R.T
X = X - X.mean(axis=0)            # center the cloud

Sigma = np.cov(X.T)              # 2x2 sample covariance matrix
w, V = np.linalg.eigh(Sigma)     # eigh: symmetric solver, ascending eigenvalues
order = np.argsort(w)[::-1]      # largest first
w, V = w[order], V[:, order]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8, 5.8))
ax.scatter(X[:, 0], X[:, 1], s=10, color=GREY, alpha=0.5)
for k, col in zip(range(2), (HILITE, ACCENT)):
    vec = V[:, k] * np.sqrt(w[k])         # scale by std dev along the axis
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), 2 * vec, color=col, lw=3.0, z=6)
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), -2 * vec, color=col, lw=3.0, z=6)
# 1-sd ellipse
tt = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 200)
ell = (V @ np.diag(np.sqrt(w)) @ np.stack([np.cos(tt), np.sin(tt)]))
ax.plot(ell[0], ell[1], color=VEC, lw=1.6, ls=":")
ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.axhline(0, color="k", lw=0.5); ax.axvline(0, color="k", lw=0.5)
ax.set_title("Covariance eigenvectors = principal axes of the cloud")
ax.set_xlabel("feature 1"); ax.set_ylabel("feature 2")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"covariance matrix:\n{Sigma.round(3)}")
print(f"eigenvalues (variances along axes): {w.round(3)}")
print(f"top eigenvector (long axis):        {V[:,0].round(3)}")
print(f"check tr(Sigma) = sum eig: {np.trace(Sigma):.3f} = {w.sum():.3f}")
```

The eigenvector for the *largest* eigenvalue lies along the cloud's long axis: it is the
single direction that captures the most variance, and projecting the data onto it is the
first principal component. The eigenvalues are the variances captured along each axis, and
their sum equals $\operatorname{tr}\Sigma$ — the total variance — confirming @thm-trace-det
on real data. This is the bridge to [GEOM-16] (why a covariance matrix is *guaranteed* an
orthonormal eigenbasis) and to Unsupervised Learning's PCA.

## Example 4 — repeated application: where the dominant eigenvector wins {#sec-ex-power}

Recall the motivating pain: understanding $A^k\mathbf x_0$ for large $k$. Eigenvectors make
the long-run behavior transparent. Consider a simple two-state transition that nudges a
population between two cities each year, encoded by a matrix $M$ acting on the state vector
$\mathbf x_k = M\mathbf x_{k-1} = M^k\mathbf x_0$. If $\mathbf x_0 = c_1\mathbf v_1 +
c_2\mathbf v_2$ in the eigenbasis, then
$$
M^k\mathbf x_0 = c_1\lambda_1^k\mathbf v_1 + c_2\lambda_2^k\mathbf v_2 .
$$
Whichever $|\lambda|$ is largest dominates as $k$ grows: the state aligns with the
**dominant eigenvector** regardless of where it started. We verify by iterating and
watching the direction converge.

```{python}
#| label: fig-power
#| fig-cap: "Repeated application drives any start toward the dominant eigenvector. A transition matrix $M$ is applied repeatedly to several different starting vectors (faint paths). All of them, normalized at each step, converge to the same direction — the eigenvector of the largest-magnitude eigenvalue (red). This is the geometry behind power iteration, steady states of Markov chains, and PageRank: the long run is governed by one eigenvector."
M = np.array([[0.85, 0.20],
              [0.15, 0.80]])   # column-stochastic-ish transition
w, V = np.linalg.eig(M)
dom = V[:, np.argmax(np.abs(w))]
dom = dom / np.linalg.norm(dom) * np.sign(dom[0])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8, 5.6))
starts = [np.array([1.0, 0.0]), np.array([0.0, 1.0]),
          np.array([1.0, 1.0]), np.array([-0.5, 1.0])]
for s in starts:
    v = s / np.linalg.norm(s)
    pts = [v.copy()]
    for _ in range(12):
        v = M @ v
        v = v / np.linalg.norm(v)
        pts.append(v.copy())
    pts = np.array(pts)
    ax.plot(pts[:, 0], pts[:, 1], "-o", color=PALE, ms=3, lw=1.2, alpha=0.9)
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), pts[0], color=GREY, lw=1.2, z=3)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), dom, color=HILITE, lw=3.0, z=6)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), -dom, color=HILITE, lw=3.0, z=6)
ax.text(*(1.05*dom), "dominant\neigenvector", color=HILITE, fontsize=10)
ax.set_xlim(-1.2, 1.2); ax.set_ylim(-1.2, 1.2); ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.axhline(0, color="k", lw=0.5); ax.axvline(0, color="k", lw=0.5)
ax.set_title("Every start converges to the dominant eigenvector")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"eigenvalues: {w.round(4)}   dominant: {w[np.argmax(np.abs(w))]:.4f}")
print(f"dominant eigenvector direction: {dom.round(4)}")
```

This is more than a curiosity: **power iteration** — repeatedly multiplying by $A$ and
renormalizing — is one of the cheapest ways to extract the dominant eigenvector of a huge
matrix, and it is precisely how the original PageRank ranked the web. The eigenvector is
not an abstraction; it is the steady state of the process.

# The honest complications {#sec-complications}

The Fundamental Theorem guaranteed $n$ eigenvalues *over the complex numbers, counted with
multiplicity*. Both of those qualifiers are warnings, and a graduate-level command of the
subject means knowing exactly what they cost. This section is the edge of applicability.

## Complex eigenvalues: a rotation has no real eigenvectors {#sec-rotation}

A pure rotation turns every direction, so geometrically it should have *no* eigenvectors —
nothing survives un-rotated. The algebra agrees by producing complex roots. Take the
$90^\circ$ rotation
$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Its characteristic polynomial, from @eq-2x2-charpoly with $a=d=0$, $b=-1$, $c=1$:
$$
p_R(\lambda) = \lambda^2 - 0\cdot\lambda + (0\cdot0 - (-1)\cdot1) = \lambda^2 + 1 .
$$
The roots solve $\lambda^2 = -1$, so $\lambda = \pm i$ — **no real eigenvalues at all**. A
real matrix can have complex eigenvalues, and when it does, it has no real eigenvectors:
geometrically it is rotating, and a rotation fixes no real direction. The complex
eigenvalues still carry meaning (their modulus is the per-step scaling and their argument is
the rotation angle), but there is no real line that the map merely stretches.

```{python}
#| label: fig-rotation
#| fig-cap: 'The honest edge case: a rotation has no real eigenvectors. Every input direction (faint) is turned $90^\circ$ by $R=\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$ (bold), so *no* arrow comes out on its own line. The characteristic polynomial $\lambda^2+1$ has roots $\pm i$ — complex, not real — which is the algebra''s way of reporting ''there is no surviving real direction.'''
R = np.array([[0.0, -1.0], [1.0, 0.0]])
thetas = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 16, endpoint=False)
units = np.stack([np.cos(thetas), np.sin(thetas)])
imgs = R @ units
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.4, 5.4))
for k in range(units.shape[1]):
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), units[:, k], color=PALE, lw=1.2, z=2)
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), imgs[:, k], color=GREY, lw=2.0, z=4)
ax.set_xlim(-1.4, 1.4); ax.set_ylim(-1.4, 1.4); ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.axhline(0, color="k", lw=0.5); ax.axvline(0, color="k", lw=0.5)
ax.set_title(r"Rotation by $90^\circ$: no direction is preserved")
plt.tight_layout()
print("eigenvalues of R:", np.linalg.eigvals(R).round(4))
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Can a real matrix have complex eigenvalues? Do they have a pattern?"* Yes — and because
the characteristic polynomial has real coefficients, **complex eigenvalues of a real matrix
always come in conjugate pairs** $a\pm bi$. (If $p(\lambda)$ has real coefficients and
$p(z)=0$, then taking the complex conjugate of the whole equation gives $p(\bar z)=0$ too.)
So a $3\times3$ real matrix has either three real eigenvalues or one real and one conjugate
pair — never exactly two complex ones. A real matrix with complex eigenvalues is doing some
rotation; it cannot be diagonalized over the reals, though it can over the complex numbers.
*Symmetric* real matrices escape this entirely — they always have real eigenvalues
([GEOM-16]).
:::

## Repeated eigenvalues and defective matrices {#sec-defective}

The second qualifier — "counted with multiplicity" — opens the gap we already saw in
@sec-ex-3x3. When an eigenvalue repeats, the question becomes: does it supply as many
independent eigenvectors as its multiplicity demands? Define the two multiplicities
introduced in @sec-fundamental:

- **algebraic multiplicity** $a_\lambda$: how many times $\lambda$ is a root of $p_A$.
- **geometric multiplicity** $g_\lambda = \dim E_\lambda$: how many independent
  eigenvectors $\lambda$ actually has.

It is always true that $1 \le g_\lambda \le a_\lambda$ (at least one eigenvector, never more
than the multiplicity). When $g_\lambda < a_\lambda$ for some $\lambda$ — strictly fewer
eigenvectors than the multiplicity demands — the matrix is **defective**: it does not have
$n$ independent eigenvectors, so its eigenvectors *cannot* form a basis for $\mathbb R^n$.

The canonical small example is the shear
$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Its characteristic polynomial is $(2-\lambda)^2$, so $\lambda=2$ with algebraic
multiplicity $2$. The eigenvectors solve $(J-2I)\mathbf v=\mathbf 0$:
$$
J - 2I = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0\end{bmatrix},
\qquad (J-2I)\mathbf v = \mathbf 0 \;\Rightarrow\; v_2 = 0 .
$$
Only $v_1$ is free, so the eigenspace is the single line spanned by
$\begin{bmatrix}1\\0\end{bmatrix}$: geometric multiplicity $1 < 2 = $ algebraic. $J$ is
defective. There is genuinely no second eigen-direction; the shear tilts everything off the
non-horizontal lines, leaving only one survivor.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**A repeated eigenvalue does not by itself mean trouble — a deficient eigenspace does.**
The identity matrix $I$ has $\lambda=1$ with algebraic multiplicity $n$, yet *every* vector
is an eigenvector ($I\mathbf v = \mathbf v$), so its geometric multiplicity is also $n$ and
it is perfectly diagonalizable (it is already diagonal). The danger is only when the
eigenspace is *smaller* than the multiplicity, $g_\lambda < a_\lambda$. Symmetric matrices
*never* suffer this — for them $g_\lambda = a_\lambda$ always ([GEOM-16]) — which is one
more reason symmetric matrices are the well-behaved ones data science relies on. Defective
matrices are why the eigendecomposition does not always exist, and why the SVD ([GEOM-20]),
which *always* exists, is the more dependable tool when you are not guaranteed symmetry.
:::

## Independence of eigenvectors from distinct eigenvalues {#sec-distinct-indep}

The bridge to the next article is one clean fact: when eigenvalues differ, their
eigenvectors are automatically independent, so distinct eigenvalues never cause a
deficiency. This is exactly what makes the "diagonalizable" question in [GEOM-15] turn
entirely on the *repeated* eigenvalues.

::: {#thm-distinct}
**Theorem (eigenvectors of distinct eigenvalues are independent).** If
$\mathbf v_1,\dots,\mathbf v_m$ are eigenvectors of $A$ with *distinct* eigenvalues
$\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_m$ (all different), then $\mathbf v_1,\dots,\mathbf v_m$ are
linearly independent.
:::

::: {.proof}
We argue by contradiction using a minimal dependent set. Suppose the vectors are
*dependent*; among all dependence relations, take one with the fewest nonzero coefficients,
say
$$
c_1\mathbf v_1 + c_2\mathbf v_2 + \cdots + c_r\mathbf v_r = \mathbf 0,
$$ {#eq-dep}
with all $c_1,\dots,c_r\neq 0$ and $r$ as small as possible. Note $r\ge 2$, since a single
eigenvector is nonzero and cannot satisfy $c_1\mathbf v_1=\mathbf 0$ with $c_1\neq0$.

Apply $A$ to @eq-dep. Using $A\mathbf v_i = \lambda_i\mathbf v_i$:
$$
c_1\lambda_1\mathbf v_1 + c_2\lambda_2\mathbf v_2 + \cdots + c_r\lambda_r\mathbf v_r
= \mathbf 0 .
$$ {#eq-dep-A}
Now multiply the original @eq-dep by $\lambda_1$ and subtract it from @eq-dep-A. The
$\mathbf v_1$ term cancels ($c_1\lambda_1 - c_1\lambda_1 = 0$), leaving
$$
c_2(\lambda_2-\lambda_1)\mathbf v_2 + \cdots + c_r(\lambda_r-\lambda_1)\mathbf v_r
= \mathbf 0 .
$$
This is a dependence relation among $\mathbf v_2,\dots,\mathbf v_r$ — *fewer* vectors than
before. Its coefficients are $c_i(\lambda_i-\lambda_1)$. Each $c_i\neq 0$ (from our
relation) and each $\lambda_i-\lambda_1\neq 0$ (the eigenvalues are distinct), so every
coefficient is nonzero. That is a nonzero dependence relation shorter than the minimal one
— a contradiction. Hence no dependence relation exists: the eigenvectors are independent.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The immediate corollary, which @sec-ex-3x3 and @sec-defective already foreshadowed:

::: {#thm-distinct-cor}
**Corollary.** If an $n\times n$ matrix has $n$ *distinct* eigenvalues, it has $n$
independent eigenvectors, which therefore form a basis of $\mathbb R^n$ (or $\mathbb C^n$).
Such a matrix is diagonalizable.
:::

This is the green light for [GEOM-15]: distinct eigenvalues guarantee a full eigenbasis.
Defectiveness can *only* arise from a repeated eigenvalue whose eigenspace is too small. The
whole subtlety of diagonalization lives in that one situation.

# How you would actually compute this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note — and here it carries a real
warning, because the by-hand method is *not* how a computer does it. Finding eigenvalues as
roots of the characteristic polynomial is numerically catastrophic for anything but the
smallest matrices: polynomial roots are wildly sensitive to tiny coefficient errors, and
forming $\det(A-\lambda I)$ symbolically is hopeless past $4\times4$. No serious library
does it that way.

In practice you call `numpy.linalg.eig(A)` (general matrices) or `numpy.linalg.eigh(A)`
(symmetric/Hermitian — faster, guaranteed real eigenvalues, orthonormal eigenvectors, and
the right tool for any covariance, Gram, or Hessian matrix). Internally these run iterative
algorithms — the **QR algorithm** and its relatives, descendants of the power iteration we
saw in @sec-ex-power — that converge to the eigenvalues without ever touching the
characteristic polynomial. `eig` returns the eigenvalues as an array and the eigenvectors as
the *columns* of a matrix, each normalized to unit length (with the sign/phase left
arbitrary, exactly the freedom flagged in @sec-find-vecs).

The characteristic polynomial remains the right tool for *understanding* and for hand
computation on $2\times2$ and $3\times3$ matrices, and the trace/determinant checks
(@thm-trace-det) are how you sanity-test any output, by hand or by machine. But when the
matrix is large, "find the roots of $\det(A-\lambda I)$" is a definition, not an algorithm.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[GEOM-13 *The Determinant*]**, which gave us the one scalar that detects
singularity — the exact tool we needed to turn "$A-\lambda I$ is singular" into the
solvable equation $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$. With eigenvalues and eigenvectors now in hand,
Movement IV continues:

- **[GEOM-15] *Diagonalization and the Eigendecomposition*** — assemble the eigenvectors
  into a matrix $V$ and the eigenvalues into a diagonal $\Lambda$ to write $A = V\Lambda
  V^{-1}$, making $A^k = V\Lambda^k V^{-1}$ and the long-run behavior of @sec-ex-power
  transparent. The defective matrices of @sec-defective are exactly the ones this cannot
  reach.
- **[GEOM-16] *Symmetric Matrices and the Spectral Theorem*** — the happy ending: symmetric
  matrices (covariance, Gram, Hessian) always have *real* eigenvalues and an *orthonormal*
  eigenbasis, escaping both complications of @sec-complications. This is the engine under
  PCA and quadratic forms.
- **[GEOM-18] *Positive-Definite Matrices*** — "all eigenvalues positive" turns out to mean
  "the quadratic form is a bowl with a unique bottom," tying eigenvalues to optimization.
- **[GEOM-26] *Condition Number and Numerical Stability*** — the ratio
  $\lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}$ predicts when a solve goes wrong; this is what
  **discharges Regression's [REGR-07]** multicollinearity prerequisite.
:::

Beyond this track, eigenvectors are the spine of PCA (Unsupervised Learning links back to
@sec-ex-cov and [GEOM-16] for the machinery), the steady-state analysis of Markov chains and
PageRank (@sec-ex-power), the stability analysis of dynamical systems, and the spectral view
of graphs.

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The geometric core.** The single picture to carry away is @fig-eigen-action: a matrix
  rotates almost every direction, and the eigenvectors are the exceptions it merely
  stretches. Everything in Movement IV is an elaboration of that one image.
- **The honest boundaries.** Two phenomena limit the eigenpicture and motivate the rest of
  the track: complex eigenvalues for rotating maps (@sec-rotation) and defective matrices
  with too few eigenvectors (@sec-defective). Symmetric matrices ([GEOM-16]) escape both,
  which is why data science leans on them so heavily; the SVD ([GEOM-20]) sidesteps both for
  *every* matrix, square or not.
- **Open threads deliberately deferred.** *How do we use the eigenvectors as a coordinate
  system?* — diagonalization, [GEOM-15]. *Why are covariance/Gram/Hessian matrices
  guaranteed real, orthogonal eigenstructure?* — the spectral theorem, [GEOM-16]. *What is
  the analogue for non-square matrices?* — singular values, [GEOM-20]. *Why does the
  eigenvalue ratio govern numerical accuracy?* — conditioning, [GEOM-26]. Each picks up a
  thread this article opened and left honestly visible.
- **Primary thread.** The numerical reality — that you never find eigenvalues by rooting
  the characteristic polynomial, but by iterative QR-type algorithms descended from the
  power iteration of @sec-ex-power — is the practical counterpart to the clean algebra here,
  and the first taste of the stability theme that closes the track in [GEOM-26].
